Name: information-theoretic_llm_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Compression of Large Language Models: Minimizing Bits-per-Weight while Preserving Task-Relevant Mutual Information
Short Hypothesis: The fundamental limit of LLM compression can be characterized by the trade-off between bits-per-weight and task-relevant mutual information, and practical algorithms can approach this limit by allocating bits to weights that maximize mutual information with the task.
Related Work: Recent work has explored mixed-precision quantization (e.g., EMQ, AMP-ViT) and adaptive compression frameworks (e.g., SNIP) but lacks a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Conditional mutual information (CMI) has been used to study generalization (Steinke & Zakynthinou, 2020), but its application to LLM compression remains unexplored. This proposal bridges these gaps by framing LLM compression as a rate-distortion problem with mutual information as the distortion measure.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are computationally expensive to deploy, prompting the need for efficient compression techniques. While existing methods focus on heuristic-based quantization and pruning, they lack a principled understanding of the fundamental limits of compression. This paper proposes an information-theoretic framework for LLM compression, characterizing the trade-off between bits-per-weight and task-relevant mutual information. We derive achievable rate-distortion bounds and introduce a practical algorithm that allocates bits to weights based on their contribution to mutual information with the task. Our approach leverages mutual information estimators (e.g., InfoNCE) and Fisher-information sensitivities to guide compression. Experiments on benchmark LLMs demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art compression ratios while preserving task performance, approaching the derived theoretical limits. This work provides a rigorous foundation for understanding and optimizing LLM compression.
Experiments: Derive achievable rate-distortion bounds for LLM compression under a mutual information distortion measure.
Develop a practical compression algorithm that allocates bits based on mutual information estimates using InfoNCE-style estimators.
Evaluate on benchmark LLMs (e.g., LLaMA, GPT-2) across tasks (e.g., language modeling, classification) to measure compression ratio vs. task performance.
Compare with state-of-the-art quantization and pruning methods (e.g., SNIP, EMQ) in terms of bits-per-weight and task accuracy.
Analyze the sensitivity of mutual information estimates to different weight subsets and validate the theoretical bounds empirically.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Mutual information estimation in high-dimensional spaces is challenging and may introduce bias or variance.
The proposed algorithm may incur additional computational overhead during bit allocation.
The theoretical bounds assume specific probabilistic models of the task and data, which may not hold in practice.
Task-specific mutual information may not generalize across diverse applications, limiting the method’s versatility.
